Row1.Pathway ID/Name: RET signaling → MAPK (RAS–RAF–MEK–ERK) (KEGG hsa04010 / generic MAPK)
Row1.Rationale: RET fusions drive ligand‑independent activation of MAPK; selective RET inhibition with selpercatinib suppresses this axis, producing high response rates and durable disease control in RET‑rearranged NSCLC.[3,5]

Row2.Rationale: RET activation engages PI3K–AKT for proliferation and survival; selpercatinib potently inhibits RET and RET‑dependent cell growth with marked selectivity over non‑RET kinases, consistent with suppression of PI3K–AKT signaling in RET‑driven tumors.[3,5]

Row3.Pathway ID/Name: RET kinase domain (on‑target) – solvent‑front/hinge mutant signaling
Row3.Rationale: Acquired RET G810C/S/R/V solvent‑front and Y806C/N hinge mutations sterically hinder selpercatinib binding, maintain RET autophosphorylation, and confer strong resistance in NSCLC and preclinical RET models.[1,2,5,8]

Row4.Rationale: MET amplification emerges in ~15–19% of post‑selpercatinib/pralsetinib lung biopsies and in multiple selpercatinib‑treated NSCLC cases; increased MET expression restores downstream MAPK/PI3K signaling despite RET blockade, mediating clinical resistance.[1,3,4,5,6]

Row5.Rationale: Acquired KRAS amplification or G12D/G12V mutations are detected in selpercatinib‑resistant RET‑fusion NSCLC; these alterations reactivate RAS–RAF–MEK–ERK signaling independent of RET, correlating with primary or acquired resistance.[1,3,5]

Row6.Pathway ID/Name: BRAF fusion–mediated MAPK activation (e.g., SKAP2::BRAF) (KEGG hsa04010)
Row6.Rationale: An acquired SKAP2::BRAF fusion in a CCDC6::RET NSCLC progressing on selpercatinib drives MEK–ERK activation as a bypass; combined selpercatinib plus MEK inhibitor trametinib produced a radiographic partial response, indicating MAPK dependence of resistance.[3,7]

Row7.Pathway ID/Name: NTRK3 fusion–TRK signaling → MAPK/PI3K (KEGG hsa04722 Trk signaling)
Row7.Rationale: Global RETgistry data and prior reports show acquired NTRK3 fusions after selpercatinib; TRK activation provides an alternative upstream driver of MAPK/PI3K signaling, reducing dependence on RET and mediating off‑target resistance.[3]

Row8.Pathway ID/Name: JAK–STAT signaling downstream of RET (KEGG hsa04630)
Row8.Rationale: RET alterations activate JAK–STAT along with MAPK and PI3K; clinical efficacy of selpercatinib across RET‑altered tumors reflects effective blockade of RET‑dependent proliferative pathways, including JAK–STAT, in oncogene‑addicted NSCLC.[3]

Row9.Rationale: RETgistry analysis identifies recurrent acquired CDKN2A/B alterations after selective RET inhibitors; disruption of CDKN2A/B‑mediated checkpoint control can promote proliferation and reduce dependence on upstream RTK signaling, contributing to resistance.[3]

Row10.Rationale: Acquired KEAP1 alterations occur in selpercatinib/pralsetinib‑resistant lung tumors; KEAP1 loss stabilizes NRF2, enhancing survival and potentially diminishing the pro‑apoptotic impact of RET–MAPK/PI3K suppression.[3]

Row11.Rationale: APC alterations are enriched post‑SRI in RET‑altered tumors; Wnt pathway activation via APC loss can provide alternative proliferative signaling and may cooperate with residual RTK activity to sustain growth under RET inhibition.[3]

Row12.Pathway ID/Name: EGFR/other RTK bypass signaling (EGFR, FGFR, other RTKs → MAPK/PI3K)
Row12.Rationale: RETgistry and review data highlight multiple off‑target RTK alterations (e.g., FGFR1 amplification, EGFR alterations) after selpercatinib/pralsetinib; these bypass tracks converge on MAPK/PI3K, allowing tumor cells to circumvent RET blockade.[3,5]
